description: How LLMs run: sampling, context windows, embeddings, KV cache, quantization.

questions.question: What does 'temperature' control in LLM sampling?
options: The physical operating temperature constraints of the GPU hardware during execution
The absolute inference latency speed of the model generation pipelines
The randomness of token selection — lower is more deterministic, higher is more varied
The computational thinking budget duration allocated to the model before it responds
questions.explanation: Temperature rescales the probability distribution over next tokens. Near 0 the model almost always picks the most likely token; higher values flatten the distribution and increase variety.

questions.question: What is 'top-p' (nucleus) sampling?
options: Configuring the sampler to pick only from the single highest-probability next token
Sampling from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability reaches p
Selecting the exact p-th rank value index next-token prediction on every step
Executing token generation processes on p parallel threads and averaging the weights
questions.explanation: Nucleus sampling keeps the smallest set of most-probable tokens whose probabilities sum to at least p (e.g., 0.9) and samples only from that 'nucleus', cutting off the unlikely tail.

questions.question: What is a model's 'context window'?
options: The historical date range and chronological timeline of text datasets that the model was trained on
The client-side browser window or layout frame where users chat with the model
The concurrent user request capacity that a single hosted model serves at once
The maximum sequence length of input and output tokens that the model can process at once
questions.explanation: The context window is the maximum sequence length — prompt plus generated output — that a model can process at once. Content beyond it is simply not visible to the model.

questions.question: What is an 'embedding' in machine learning?
options: A high-dimensional vector representation capturing the semantic meaning of text, images, or audio
A system design pattern for nesting a model directly inside another local application
A highly compressed backup copy of the training data stored on localized disk volumes
The specific HTML and Javascript code templates used to embed and style an interactive chatbot interface on an enterprise webpage
questions.explanation: Embeddings map text (or images, audio, etc.) into high-dimensional numeric vectors where semantically similar items end up close together — the foundation of semantic search.

questions.question: How does byte-pair encoding (BPE), a common tokenization method, build its vocabulary?
options: It indexes natural language words and assigns a single unique integer token per dictionary word
It iteratively merges the most frequent pairs of symbols in the training corpus into new tokens
It tokenizes raw text payloads by splitting inputs at every whitespace or punctuation character only
It relies on crowdsourced human annotators to define and validate semantic boundary tags for tokens
questions.explanation: BPE starts from small units (bytes or characters) and repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pairs, producing a vocabulary of subword units that balances coverage and efficiency.

questions.question: What is a model's 'knowledge cutoff'?
options: The threshold capacity limiting the total number of facts the model can store
The specific condition or end token where the model ceases to generate text
An approximate point through which the model's pre-training data substantially represent events and information
A safety filter configuration designed to redact copyrighted text snippets
questions.explanation: A knowledge cutoff summarizes the approximate recency of a model's pre-training knowledge. It is not a hard guarantee that every earlier event is known or that no later information entered through fine-tuning, system updates, the prompt, retrieval, or tools.

questions.question: What does it mean that most LLMs generate text 'autoregressively'?
options: They process inputs and produce the complete output response string in a single, parallel step
They generate output one token at a time, with each new token prediction conditioned on all previously generated tokens
They calculate their target response arrays in reverse token order and then flip them before returning the final text to the UI
They mathematically regress their output token probability distributions toward the absolute statistical average seen in training data
questions.explanation: Autoregressive generation predicts the next token given everything so far, appends it, and repeats — which is why responses stream out token by token.

questions.question: What is the KV cache used for during LLM inference?
options: Storing historical conversation databases on local disks for later pipeline validation runs and offline tuning
Caching complete generated text responses to frequently encountered user prompt queries to bypass model inference passes entirely
Storing the attention key and value tensors of previous tokens so they aren't recomputed for each new token
Keeping an active cache copy of the network parameter weights inside RAM memory to prevent disk access latency
questions.explanation: In autoregressive decoding, each new token attends to all previous tokens. The KV cache stores the already-computed key/value projections so each step only computes attention for the new token, dramatically speeding up generation at the cost of memory.

questions.question: What is 'quantization' of a model?
options: Reducing the numeric precision of weights (e.g., 16-bit to 4-bit) to cut memory and compute costs
Splitting the transformer execution tasks across multiple experimental quantum computers
Evaluating and counting the exact parameters and node layers configured inside the network
Applying advanced cryptographic encryption keys to model weight parameter files so they cannot be copied or reverse-engineered by developers
questions.explanation: Quantization stores weights (and sometimes activations) at lower numeric precision, such as INT8 or 4-bit formats. It shrinks memory footprint and speeds inference, usually with a small accuracy trade-off.

questions.question: What is 'knowledge distillation'?
options: Executing post-training compliance routines to identify and delete false facts from frozen model weights
Training a smaller, resource-efficient 'student' model to reproduce the logits and behavior of a larger 'teacher' model
Reconstructively extracting original copyrighted training data files back out of the frozen model parameters
Merging and averaging parameters from two distinct model layers of the exact same size into a single network
questions.explanation: Distillation transfers capability from a large teacher model to a smaller student by training the student on the teacher's outputs, yielding cheaper models that retain much of the performance.
